titles.title: CyberLLMInstruct: A Pseudo-malicious Dataset Revealing Safety-performance Trade-offs in Cyber Security LLM Fine-tuning

descriptions.description: This poster presents CyberLLMInstruct, a dataset of 54,928 pseudo-malicious instruction-response pairs compiled from CTF challenges, academic papers, industry reports, and CVE databases. The study evaluates how fine-tuning seven open-source LLMs on this dataset dramatically improves cyber security task performance (up to 92.50% accuracy on CyberMetric benchmark) but severely compromises safety resilience against OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities, particularly prompt injection. Results demonstrate a clear, quantifiable trade-off between performance and safety, with Llama 3.1 8B's security score dropping from 0.95 to 0.15 against prompt injection after fine-tuning.

Dataset compiled from CTF challenges, academic papers, industry reports, and CVE databases across 8 security categories. Comprehensive evaluation using seven open-source LLMs (Phi 3 Mini 3.8B, Mistral 7B, Owen 2.5 7B, Llama 3 8B, Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B, Llama 2 70B) measuring performance with CyberMetric benchmark (80Q, 500Q, 2kQ, 10kQ) and safety with DeepEval against OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities.
Presented at the ACM Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Security (AISec) 2025, co-located with ACM CCS 2025.

conference.conferenceName: ACM Workshop on Artificial Intelligence and Security 2025

sections.sectionContent:
Problem Statement: LLMs are being integrated into cyber security for tasks like malware analysis and threat detection. However, fine-tuning them for these specific tasks may introduce critical safety vulnerabilities.

Research Gap: There is a lack of comprehensive datasets and evaluations that expose the trade-off between task performance and safety resilience in LLMs fine-tuned for cyber security.

Objective: To introduce the CyberLLMInstruct dataset and evaluate how fine-tuning impacts both the performance and safety of LLMs in a cyber security context.

Our research introduces CyberLLMInstruct, a dataset of 54,928 pseudo-malicious instruction-response pairs.

Dataset Creation: compiled from diverse sources, including Capture the Flag (CTF) challenges, academic papers, industry reports, and Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVE) databases, covering a wide range of cyber security tasks.

Evaluation: comprehensive evaluation using seven open-source LLMs, measuring performance with CyberMetric and safety with DeepEval.

Dataset categories: Malware (19,234), Social Engineering (13,732), DoS/DDoS (5,493), MITM (3,493), Zero-Day (4,394), Password (3,296), IoT (1,648), Injection (1,648). Total: 54,928 records.

Significant performance gains, with models achieving up to 92.50% accuracy on the CyberMetric benchmark (Llama 3.1 8B at 80Q).

Fine-tuning an LLM to be highly proficient in cyber security tasks consistently led to decreased security scores across all vulnerability categories. For example, Llama 3.1 8B's security score dropped from 0.95 to 0.15 against prompt injection.

Fine-tuning also reduced the inference efficiency for all models.

Model size and architecture affect safety resilience following fine-tuning, with the effect varying across attack categories.

There is a clear, quantifiable trade-off between performance and safety.

Future Work: Develop new fine-tuning methodologies that can effectively balance performance gains with the preservation of safety and resilience. Ablation analysis on different categories of cyber security data to understand how specific types of content, such as malware-related or social engineering data, affect model safety.

imageCaptions.caption: Figure 1. Security categories in CyberLLMInstruct dataset. Pie chart showing distribution: Malware (35%), Social Engineering (25%), DoS/DDoS (10%), MITM (8%), Zero-Day (8%), Password (6%), IoT (3%), Injection (3%).

Figure 2. A high-level overview of the dataset creation process, showing pipeline from data gathering through preliminary filtering, formatting into Q&A pairs, security AI-assisted alignment, to final dataset assembly.

Figure 3. Abstraction of dual impacts of LLMs in cyber security, illustrating both defensive (vulnerability detection, incident response, threat intelligence) and offensive (phishing, malware generation, attack automation) uses.
Figure 4. Performance of base (green) and fine-tuned (red) LLMs against OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities.
Figure 5. Execution times for base and fine-tuned LLMs.
Figure 6. Absolute difference before and after fine-tuning across vulnerability categories and model architectures.

tableCaptions.caption: Table 1. Comparison of CyberLLMInstruct with other cyber security datasets including CyBERTuned, CySecBERT, SecQA, CyberMetric, and SVEN across scope, malicious content, instruction format, size, security testing, and primary use dimensions.
Table 2. Accuracy results (%) for different base (before arrow) and fine-tuned (after arrow) LLMs on the CyberMetric benchmark at 80Q, 500Q, 2kQ, and 10kQ question counts.
